 I sat across from my friend last week. She was crying. Again. Telling me about another man who promis...
htmlContent:
The part that keeps choosing unavailable partners

I sat across from my friend last week. She was crying. Again. Telling me about another man who promised her the moon and then disappeared when she needed him most. And I felt it in my chest. That familiar ache. That knowing.

Because I've been her. I've been you. I've been the one who looks at a person who is clearly, obviously, painfully unavailable and thinks "maybe this time will be different."

Here's the thing. That part of you that keeps choosing unavailable partners? It's not stupid. It's not broken. It's not trying to ruin your life. It's trying to protect you. It's just using an old map for a new territory. And that map is worn thin from years of use.

Stay with me here. I want to talk about why this happens. Not from a place of shame or blame. From a place of understanding. From the messy, complicated reality of what it means to have a nervous system that learned love looks a certain way.

The blueprint your nervous system learned

When you grow up with emotional neglect or inconsistency, your brain builds a template. Bessel van der Kolk, the psychiatrist who wrote "The Body Keeps the Score," talks about this extensively. Your nervous system learns what feels familiar. And here's the cruel trick: familiar doesn't mean good. Familiar means recognizable. Familiar means predictable, even if the prediction is pain.

Think about it. If your parents were hot and cold, loving one moment and distant the next, that pattern gets wired into your attachment system. Your body learns that love feels like waiting. Love feels like proving yourself. Love feels like never quite being enough.

So when you meet someone who is clear, consistent, and available, something feels wrong. It feels boring. It feels suspicious. You might even find yourself thinking "where's the catch?" Because your nervous system is scanning for the familiar pattern. And the familiar pattern is chaos.

Look, I know this is heavy material. And I know there's a part of you that might be thinking "this is too much" or "I can't handle this" or "maybe I should just close this tab." That part is trying to protect you. And that's okay. But I want you to know that you can handle this. You've already handled worse. The fact that you're here, reading this, means your survival system did its job. You made it.

What we're doing here isn't about reliving the past. It's about understanding it well enough to stop being controlled by it. There's a difference between being trapped in a memory and looking at it from a safe distance. You get to choose how close you get. You get to choose when to step back. That choice - that agency - is itself a form of healing.

I remember dating a man who called when he said he would. Who showed up on time. Who asked about my day and actually listened. And I felt nothing. I felt numb. I felt bored. I ended it and went back to someone who would text me at 2am and then ignore me for three days. Because that felt like something. That felt like love. That felt like home.

Wild, right? We literally choose the pain we know over the peace we don't understand.

For understanding emotional neglect specifically, a helpful resource is Running on Empty (paid link). It explains how growing up with emotional emptiness creates this blueprint for seeking connection in people who can't give it.

The protector part that keeps you safe from intimacy

Richard Schwartz, the creator of Internal Family Systems, would call this a protector part. A part of you that has a job. And that job is to keep you from getting hurt again. Choosing someone unavailable is a brilliant strategy, if you think about it.

If you choose someone who can't really be there, you never have to risk real intimacy. You never have to be seen fully. You never have to face the terror of being loved and then losing it. Because you never really had it in the first place.

This part learned early that closeness equals danger. That wanting someone means eventually losing them. That needing someone means they will use that need against you. So it picks people who are already half out the door. People who have one foot in, one foot out. People who are married to their work, their addiction, their ex, their fear of commitment.

Something I want to name explicitly. If you're reading this and thinking "this doesn't apply to me because my trauma wasn't bad enough" - I hear you. And I want to challenge that thought gently. Trauma isn't measured by what happened to you. It's measured by what happened inside you. Two people can experience the same event and have completely different nervous system responses. Neither is wrong. Neither is "too sensitive." Your response was your response. It was real. It counts.

Gabor Mate says it perfectly: "Trauma is not what happens to you. It's what happens inside you as a result of what happened to you." So if your body carries the imprint of something - if your nervous system is dysregulated, if your relationships are affected, if your sense of self was shaped by it - then it matters. Regardless of how it looks from the outside. Regardless of whether anyone else would call it "trauma." Your body doesn't lie.

And then you get to spend all your energy trying to win them over. Trying to be good enough. Trying to be the one who finally breaks through their walls. Sound familiar?

It's exhausting. Honestly, it's exhausting just writing about it. But here's what I want you to see: that part is not your enemy. It's a scared child who learned that the only way to survive was to never really show up. To never really ask for what you need. To never risk being too much.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) by Pete Walker. He talks directly about the fawn response and how we learn to abandon ourselves in relationships.

Why your body picks the wrong people before your mind catches up

Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed Polyvagal Theory, would tell you that your nervous system is making these choices before your conscious mind even gets a vote. Your vagus nerve is reading micro-expressions, tone of voice, body language, and energy. It's scanning for safety or danger. And it's using your childhood as the reference point.

So when you meet someone who is aloof, your body might actually feel more relaxed. Because aloof is familiar. Aloof means you know the rules. You know how to be around aloof. You've been practicing aloof your whole life.

But when you meet someone who is warm and present and consistent, your body might go into alarm. Because warm is unfamiliar. Warm means you don't know the rules. Your nervous system says "danger, this is new, we don't know how to handle this."

Let me tell you about the protector parts. The ones that look like anger, or control, or perfectionism, or emotional walls. In IFS, we call them managers and firefighters. Managers try to prevent pain by controlling everything. Firefighters try to extinguish pain after it's already been triggered - through numbing, bingeing, dissociating, raging.

I used to hate my protector parts. The part that made me cold and distant. The part that made me work until I collapsed. The part that drank too much. I thought they were the problem. Turns out they were the solution - the best solution my system could find at the time. They kept me alive when nothing else could.

Notice if there's a part of you that's skeptical right now. A part that says "this is too simple" or "this won't work for me" or "I'm too damaged for this." That's a protector too. It's trying to save you from hope, because hope has been dangerous before. It's been followed by disappointment too many times. Can you thank that part? Can you say "I get it. You're trying to protect me from getting hurt again." That's not weakness. That's the beginning of internal trust.

And then you get that feeling in your gut. The one that says "something's off." Except it's not off. It's just different. Your body is lying to you. Not out of malice. Out of protection.

Notice what just happened in your body when you read that. Did your chest tighten? Did your stomach clench? Did you feel a wave of recognition? That's the part. That's the one we're talking about.

A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible is Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link) by Nedra Glover Tawwab. It helps you understand why boundaries feel so dangerous when your nervous system is used to enmeshment or neglect.

How to start making different choices without hating the part that made the old ones

I'm not going to tell you to just "choose better." That's toxic positivity dressed up as advice. It ignores the very real nervous system patterns that keep you stuck. It ignores the fact that your body is literally wired to seek what it knows.

What I will tell you is this: start by noticing. Just notice. When you feel that pull toward someone who is clearly unavailable, don't judge yourself. Don't shame yourself. Just say "oh, there's that part. I see you. I know you're trying to protect me."

Then get curious. Ask that part what it's afraid would happen if you chose someone who was truly available. Listen to the answer. It might be terrified. It might say "they'll leave anyway." It might say "you don't deserve someone good." It might say "if you let yourself love someone, you'll lose yourself."

Janina Fisher's work on structural dissociation helped me understand something crucial. After trauma, the personality doesn't just get "damaged" - it fragments. Not in a dramatic, movie-style way. In a quiet, everyday way. The part that goes to work and smiles at colleagues. The part that cries in the shower. The part that goes numb when someone gets too close. These aren't masks. They're survival adaptations.

I lived like this for years without knowing it. Switching between states so smoothly that no one noticed. Not even me. The high-functioning part handled everything visible. The wounded parts only came out when I was alone, or drunk, or so exhausted that the walls came down. Sound familiar?

Here's what I want you to know. Those parts don't need to be eliminated. They need to be heard. They need to know that the danger is over, that they can put down the weapons they've been carrying. But they won't believe it just because you tell them. They need to feel it. In the body. In repeated experiences of safety. It takes time. It takes patience. It takes more self-compassion than most of us know how to give.

Those are old tapes. They were recorded in a different time, for a different situation. They kept you safe then. But they're keeping you small now.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link). It gives you concrete exercises for working with these protector parts.

The goal isn't to get rid of this part. The goal is to update its job description. To help it see that you're an adult now. That you have resources. That you can survive rejection. That you can handle intimacy. That you are not the same helpless child who needed those defenses.

And that takes time. It takes practice. It takes choosing the boring, consistent person and sitting with the discomfort of not feeling the spark. It takes staying when everything in you wants to run. It takes letting yourself be loved in a way you don't fully understand yet.

I'm not there yet. I still feel the pull. I still have moments where I'm drawn to the person who is emotionally unavailable, the one who keeps me guessing, the one who makes me work for scraps of attention. But I'm better at noticing now. And sometimes, just sometimes, I choose differently.

That's enough. That's progress. That's healing.

Healing Journey Resources

  • The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link)
  • Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link)
  • Running on Empty (paid link)
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link)
